title: Practice Exam #2 (Cloud Database Engineer)
description: Google Cloud Certified Professional Cloud Database Engineer - 50 questions

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that stores patient billing transactions updated throughout the day. After a recent incident where an automated script accidentally overwrote critical records, the engineering team needs the ability to restore the database to any specific second within the past seven days to reverse such errors. What should you do to meet this requirement?

options.text: Enable automated backups and binary logging on the instance
options.explanation:
Point-in-time recovery on Cloud SQL requires both automated backups and binary logging working together. Automated backups provide periodic full snapshots of the database, while binary logging captures every individual data change between those snapshots. When a recovery request is made to a precise timestamp, Cloud SQL restores the most recent backup taken before that moment and then replays the binary logs forward to the exact second specified. This is the only approach that allows granular temporal recovery down to the second, which is exactly what the team needs to undo accidental data overwrites.
Setting a retention window on automated backups alone only lets you restore to the exact times when those backups were captured. If the script corrupted data two hours after the last backup, those two hours of valid transactions before the corruption would be unrecoverable. A cross-region read replica is designed to distribute read traffic geographically and provide disaster recovery against regional outages, but it replicates data in near real time, meaning corrupted or overwritten records would propagate to the replica almost immediately. A high-availability failover instance protects against infrastructure failures like a zone going down by automatically switching to a standby, but it mirrors the primary database continuously and offers no mechanism to roll back to a previous point in time when bad data was written by application logic.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company runs a Cloud SQL database that powers a patient scheduling platform used across multiple states. The platform experiences sudden surges in usage during flu season and other health events. Leadership has mandated that the database architecture must withstand a full regional failure and that the team must be able to validate the disaster recovery workflow in a safe manner that does not impact production workloads. The solution must also align with aggressive RPO and RTO commitments outlined in the company's service level agreements. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.text: Deploy a cross-region failover replica and test database cloning
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to set up a cross-region read replica and then validate the recovery process by testing its promotion. A cross-region read replica maintains a continuously updated copy of the primary database in a geographically separate region, which directly addresses the requirement to survive a complete regional outage. When the replica is promoted, it becomes a fully independent primary instance capable of serving all read and write traffic. This promotion can be tested without disrupting the original production database, which satisfies the mandate to validate the disaster recovery workflow safely. The approach also supports the aggressive RPO and RTO targets because the replica stays nearly synchronized with the primary and can be promoted relatively quickly during an actual emergency.
A zonal standby instance only protects against failures within a single zone and would not help during a broader regional outage, making it inadequate given the stated requirements. Point-in-time recovery is useful when recovering from data corruption or accidental deletions, but it does not serve as a high-availability mechanism during a regional disaster. Configuring an external replica in a separate VPC introduces significant networking complexity that could slow down recovery efforts and does not leverage the native Cloud SQL replication and promotion capabilities. Testing instance migration in that context adds operational overhead that is not aligned with a streamlined failover path. Deploying a cross-region failover replica and testing database cloning conflates two different processes, since cloning creates an independent copy of the database at a point in time rather than verifying the actual failover mechanism that would be invoked during a real outage. The most direct and verifiable path to meeting both the resilience and testing requirements is through cross-region read replica promotion.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company manages patient appointment data in Cloud SQL and stores billing records in Cloud Spanner. Both systems are hosted on Google Cloud. The analytics team needs to build dashboards and generate reports using specific fields from these databases, but company policy prohibits giving analysts direct login credentials to any production database. The team also wants to minimize infrastructure overhead and avoid maintaining separate copies of the data. The company already has BigQuery as its central analytics platform and wants analysts to query everything through BigQuery while ensuring only approved tables and columns are visible. What should the data engineering team do?

options.text: Configure the analytics tools used by the team to connect directly to the production databases using read-only database roles limited to specific schemas
options.explanation:
The best approach is to use BigQuery federated queries through the EXTERNAL_QUERY function wrapped inside BigQuery views. BigQuery supports federated queries against external databases including Cloud SQL and Cloud Spanner, which means data can be queried in place without moving it. By defining views in BigQuery that contain EXTERNAL_QUERY statements targeting only the approved tables and columns, the team creates a controlled access layer. Analysts receive permissions on the views rather than on the underlying databases, which satisfies the policy against sharing production database credentials. This design keeps BigQuery as the single analytics interface while avoiding any data duplication or synchronization pipelines. The views themselves enforce column-level restrictions so that only the intended fields are exposed.
Setting up scheduled pipelines to copy data into BigQuery introduces duplicated datasets that need ongoing maintenance and refresh cycles, which directly contradicts the requirement to avoid maintaining separate copies. Building read replicas in separate Cloud SQL and Cloud Spanner instances adds infrastructure management burden and still requires issuing database credentials to analysts, violating the company policy. Configuring analytics tools to connect directly to the production databases exposes those systems to additional client traffic and requires granting database-level credentials, which the company explicitly prohibits. That approach also bypasses BigQuery as the centralized analytics platform. Only the federated query approach through BigQuery views meets all of the stated constraints simultaneously.

questions.text: A multinational financial services firm is developing a real-time payments platform that must process transactions simultaneously from data centers across North America, Europe, and Asia. The system requires a unified database layer that delivers strongly consistent reads and writes regardless of which region initiates the transaction. The platform must also handle increasing transaction volumes by scaling out without sacrificing ACID guarantees. Which Google Cloud database should the firm select?

options.text: Firestore in Native mode deployed across multiple regions
options.explanation:
Cloud Spanner in a multi-region configuration is the right choice here because it provides a single logical relational database that synchronously replicates data across geographically separated regions. This means every transaction, no matter which continent it originates from, benefits from strong consistency and full ACID semantics. The service scales horizontally by adding nodes, and this scaling does not compromise transactional integrity. It was designed specifically to handle globally distributed workloads where consistent behavior across all regions is non-negotiable, which aligns perfectly with a real-time payments platform operating across three continents.
Cloud SQL is a managed relational database that supports transactions within a single primary instance, but its cross-region replicas are read-only and asynchronous, so it cannot provide strongly consistent writes from multiple regions simultaneously. Firestore in Native mode offers automatic multi-region replication and works well with document-oriented data, but it lacks the relational transactional semantics needed for complex multi-row financial operations spanning a global deployment. Bigtable excels at high-throughput analytical and time-series workloads and can route traffic across clusters in different regions, but it does not support multi-row ACID transactions, making it unsuitable when strong transactional consistency is a firm requirement.

questions.text: Your logistics company maintains shipment tracking data in Amazon DynamoDB. Due to increasing data volumes and plans to build real-time analytics dashboards on Google Cloud, your team has been asked to migrate this data to Cloud Bigtable. The solution should minimize downtime during the transition, ensure that both databases remain consistent throughout the migration period, and avoid unnecessary infrastructure management. What should you do?

options.text: Build a Dataflow pipeline using the DynamoDBIO and BigtableIO connectors to replicate data from DynamoDB to Bigtable in near real time
options.explanation:
The best approach is to create a managed Dataflow pipeline that uses the DynamoDBIO and BigtableIO connectors to continuously synchronize data between the two systems. Dataflow is a fully managed service that handles autoscaling and fault tolerance without requiring you to provision or manage infrastructure. Because it supports streaming ingestion, new and updated records in DynamoDB can be propagated to Bigtable with very low latency, which keeps both databases consistent during the migration window. This dramatically reduces downtime since both systems can serve traffic while data is being replicated. The purpose-built connectors reduce the amount of custom code needed and simplify error handling throughout the pipeline.
Exporting DynamoDB tables to JSON files and importing them with the cbt tool is a batch-oriented approach that does not maintain ongoing consistency between the two databases and would require repeated manual exports or scheduled downtime. Writing a custom application with both client libraries introduces significant development and maintenance overhead, along with a higher risk of bugs or partial failures during the replication process. Using a Dataproc cluster to move data in batch adds cluster provisioning and management complexity that conflicts with the goal of minimal operational overhead, and it does not natively support continuous near real-time synchronization the way a streaming Dataflow pipeline does.

questions.text: A national healthcare analytics platform stores patient monitoring data and clinical research datasets in a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database with 16 vCPUs and 64 GB of RAM. Research teams execute complex analytical queries across tables containing hundreds of millions of lab results and vitals records to identify treatment outcome patterns, which regularly pushes CPU utilization above 85% and causes latency spikes in the patient monitoring dashboard. The database simultaneously handles continuous writes from thousands of connected medical devices alongside frequent reads from clinical staff reviewing real-time patient status. The organization is preparing to onboard several new hospital networks and must ensure that research analytics never degrade the responsiveness of live patient monitoring. You need an architecture that delivers elastic horizontal read scaling and purpose-built acceleration of large-scale analytical scans. What should you implement?

options.text: Transition to a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL High Availability configuration with 32 vCPUs and integrated PgBouncer
options.explanation:
The best architecture here is AlloyDB for PostgreSQL with its columnar engine and read pool capabilities. AlloyDB is purpose-built to handle hybrid transactional and analytical processing (HTAP) workloads, which is exactly the challenge described in this scenario. The columnar engine maintains a memory-resident columnar data format that dramatically speeds up the aggregations, joins, and scans that the research teams are running across hundreds of millions of rows. Read pools allow the analytical query load to be distributed across horizontally scalable read-only instances that operate independently from the primary, ensuring that patient monitoring writes and reads remain fast and unaffected by heavy research queries. This combination of workload isolation and analytical acceleration is what makes AlloyDB the right fit as the platform scales to new hospital networks.
Upgrading SSD storage and adding materialized views addresses only a narrow slice of the problem. Materialized views can speed up specific predefined queries but do not provide general-purpose analytical acceleration or elastic scaling, and more storage does nothing to resolve CPU contention between workloads. Using an Enterprise Plus Cloud SQL instance with read replicas can help distribute read traffic, but those replicas process data using the same row-oriented engine and lack the specialized columnar processing needed to efficiently handle massive analytical scans. Switching to a Cloud SQL High Availability setup with more vCPUs and PgBouncer improves connection management and failover resilience, but it does not separate analytical workloads from transactional ones and does nothing to accelerate complex queries on large datasets. None of these alternatives deliver both the workload isolation and the analytical scan acceleration that the scenario demands.

questions.text: A SaaS company hosts its multi-tenant backend on Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. The engineering team has noticed that database costs have been climbing steadily, but they are unsure which tenant workloads or application features are responsible for the increased resource consumption. Leadership wants a repeatable approach that pinpoints the most resource-intensive database operations so the team can right-size the Cloud SQL instance and avoid paying for unnecessary capacity. What should you do?

options.text: Use the Recommender API to automatically apply suggested changes to the database configuration flags
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage Query Insights, the native Cloud SQL feature that surfaces per-query performance data such as CPU time, execution count, and latency. Because the engineering team needs to understand which specific tenant workloads or application features are driving up costs, they require visibility into the actual SQL statements hitting the database rather than just aggregate infrastructure metrics. Query Insights provides exactly this level of granularity, allowing the team to correlate resource consumption with individual queries. Once they know which operations are the heaviest, they can decide whether to optimize those queries, restructure application logic, or adjust the instance size to match what the workload truly demands. This creates a repeatable, data-driven process rather than one based on guesswork.
Building custom Python scripts to parse logs and trigger scaling functions introduces unnecessary complexity, maintenance burden, and does not help the team understand which queries are actually expensive. Cloud Monitoring alerts tied to CPU utilization only tell you that the instance is busy, not why it is busy, so upgrading the machine type in response is a reactive measure that often leads to over-provisioning because teams rarely remember to scale back down afterward. The Recommender API can offer useful suggestions about configuration changes, but it cannot autonomously modify instance settings without additional automation, and it does not provide the query-level detail needed to trace costs back to specific database operations.

questions.text: A gaming company is building a real-time leaderboard and player state tracking system on Google Cloud. The system must deliver sub-millisecond response times for read and write operations against an in-memory key-value store. The total dataset is expected to grow beyond 600 GB as the player base scales globally. The engineering team also requires that the database be accessible from their application VPC through an endpoint-based connectivity model that does not involve VPC peering with the managed service network. Which architecture should the team implement?

options.text: Memorystore Redis Cluster using Private Service Connect
options.explanation:
The cluster-based Redis offering on Memorystore is the right choice here because it satisfies both the large dataset requirement and the networking constraint. A Redis Cluster distributes data across multiple shards, enabling horizontal scaling well beyond 600 GB while preserving sub-millisecond latency that Redis is known for. Private Service Connect creates an endpoint within the application VPC, allowing the gaming platform to reach the managed Redis service without establishing VPC peering. This avoids CIDR overlap risks and provides a cleaner network boundary compared to Private Service Access.
A standard Memorystore Redis Instance has a maximum memory ceiling that cannot accommodate a 600 GB dataset, so even if the networking model were correct, it would fail on capacity. Pairing a standard instance with Private Service Connect still does not resolve the storage limitation, since the architecture is not designed to scale horizontally across nodes. Cloud Bigtable is a wide-column NoSQL database that can handle large datasets, but it is not an in-memory key-value cache and does not natively support Redis-compatible operations, which would require significant application rework. Memorystore Redis Instance with Private Service Access uses VPC peering under the hood, which directly contradicts the requirement to avoid peering with the service provider network. Only the cluster topology paired with the endpoint-based connectivity model meets every stated requirement.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm runs an AlloyDB for PostgreSQL cluster that stores patient billing records. The operations team currently relies on automated daily backups with a standard retention window. After a recent audit, the compliance officer has mandated that the organization must be able to recover the database to a state no older than 4 hours prior to any failure event. What change should you make to the AlloyDB cluster to satisfy this recovery mandate?

options.text: Configure the automated backup schedule to run every 4 hours
options.explanation:
Activating continuous backup on the AlloyDB cluster is the right approach to meet a strict recovery point objective of 4 hours or less. Continuous backup works by constantly archiving write-ahead log data to Cloud Storage, which enables point-in-time recovery down to a specific second within the configured recovery window. Because every database change is captured as it occurs, the gap between the last recoverable state and any failure is effectively reduced to seconds rather than hours. This level of granularity ensures the organization can always restore billing records to a point well within the 4-hour threshold demanded by the compliance officer.
Extending the backup retention period to 90 days only determines how far back in time old backup snapshots are preserved and has no impact on how frequently new backup data is captured. Scheduling automated backups every 4 hours would create periodic snapshots, but a failure occurring just before the next scheduled backup could still result in losing nearly 4 hours of transactions, and this approach lacks the fine-grained recovery precision that log-based archiving provides. Deploying a cross-region read replica improves read availability and can help with disaster recovery scenarios, but it does not directly address the recovery point objective because the underlying backup cadence would remain unchanged without continuous backup enabled.

questions.text: A multinational e-commerce company has recently migrated its product catalog and transaction analytics database to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL. During seasonal sales events like Black Friday, the platform sees dramatic increases in read-heavy reporting queries that power merchant dashboards and inventory analytics. You need to architect a solution that maintains dashboard responsiveness during these peak shopping periods while keeping infrastructure costs under control during normal business operations. What approach should you take?

options.text: Route all reporting and analytical queries to the primary read-write instance to reduce architectural complexity
options.explanation:
- The best approach is to dynamically adjust the read pool node count in response to actual workload signals. AlloyDB separates its read pools from the primary instance, and because all nodes share the same underlying storage layer, adding read pool nodes is a lightweight way to boost query concurrency without duplicating data. During seasonal sales events, you can increase the node count to handle the surge in reporting queries, and then scale back down afterward so you are not paying for unused compute. This cloud-native elasticity directly ties your spending to real demand, which is exactly what the scenario requires.
Sending all analytical queries to the primary instance creates resource contention between reads and writes, which risks degrading transactional performance during the most critical revenue-generating periods. Keeping a permanently oversized read pool guarantees capacity but ignores the cost optimization requirement, since those nodes sit idle during the vast majority of the year when traffic is normal. Changing the machine type of individual nodes is a vertical scaling strategy that is less flexible than horizontal scaling and cannot provide the same fine-grained ability to match capacity to fluctuating demand. It also typically requires node restarts and does not address the need to reduce costs when demand subsides.

questions.text: A financial services company operates a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database as its primary transactional store in europe-west1. To support their disaster recovery strategy, they maintain a cross-region read replica in europe-west3. As part of their quarterly DR drill, the database engineering team needs to confirm that the distant replica environment can assume full read-write responsibilities if the primary region experiences a complete and prolonged outage. They want to validate this with the least possible data loss. What approach best achieves this objective?

options.text: Initiate a manual failover to the high availability standby node located in an alternate zone within the same region
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to use Cloud SQL's replica promotion capability to convert the cross-region read replica into a fully independent primary database. When a cross-region replica is promoted, it severs the replication link and becomes a standalone instance capable of handling both reads and writes. This is the definitive way to validate a regional disaster recovery procedure because it tests the actual infrastructure that would be relied upon during a real outage. Promoting the replica also allows the team to verify application connectivity, DNS or connection string updates, and data consistency in the secondary region under realistic conditions. This method offers the least data loss because the replica has been continuously receiving replicated changes from the primary up until the moment of promotion.
Initiating a failover to a high availability standby only moves the workload to a different zone within the same region, which does not help if the entire region is down and therefore does not validate cross-region recovery readiness. Restoring from an automated backup into a new instance in the secondary region is a legitimate recovery technique, but it is much slower than promoting an already-running replica and introduces a larger window of potential data loss since backups are periodic rather than continuous. It also fails to test the replica infrastructure that was specifically set up to handle this scenario. Deleting the primary instance is a destructive action that would trigger an intra-region failover to a zonal standby rather than redirecting traffic to a replica in another region, making it both dangerous and ineffective as a cross-region DR validation method.

questions.text: A healthcare technology startup is building a patient appointment scheduling platform on Google Cloud Spanner. The system needs to handle thousands of concurrent appointment bookings per second across multiple regions. The existing data model uses several reference tables that do not have a naturally unique column, and the development team wants to avoid write performance degradation as the system scales. What approach should the team take when designing the primary keys?

options.text: Use a monotonically increasing integer column as the primary key to simplify ordering
options.explanation:
The best approach is to generate universally unique identifiers using the GENERATE_UUID function and store them in a STRING(36) column as the primary key. Cloud Spanner distributes data across nodes based on the sorted order of primary key values. When keys are random and uniformly distributed, write operations spread evenly across all available nodes, which prevents any single node from becoming overwhelmed. Since the reference tables lack a natural unique column, a UUID serves double duty by providing both uniqueness and optimal write distribution. This is especially important in a high-throughput scenario like concurrent appointment bookings across regions, where even minor hot-spotting can cascade into significant latency problems.
Cloud Spanner requires every table to have a primary key defined at creation time, so the option of creating tables without one is not technically possible. The suggestion to add a primary key after loading data also fails because Spanner needs the key structure in place before any rows can be inserted, as the key determines how data is physically partitioned and stored from the very first write. Using a monotonically increasing integer as the primary key would cause all new rows to be written to the same node at the tail end of the key range, which is exactly the hot-spotting problem the team is trying to avoid. Sequential values like auto-incrementing integers or timestamps concentrate writes rather than distributing them, making this the opposite of what a distributed database like Spanner needs to perform well at scale.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics platform stores patient billing records in a Cloud SQL for MySQL Enterprise Plus instance. An internal audit policy mandates that database administrators must be able to restore the database to any precise second within a rolling 30-day window. What is the correct way to configure this instance?

options.text: Enable binary logging and adjust database flags to preserve undo logs for a 30-day period
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to enable point-in-time recovery and extend the transaction log retention to cover the full 30-day window. Point-in-time recovery in Cloud SQL relies on binary logs that record every change made to the database, allowing you to replay transactions up to a specific second. The Enterprise Plus edition defaults to retaining these logs for seven days, so you must explicitly increase the transactionLogRetentionDays setting to 30 to satisfy the audit requirement. Once configured, the system continuously captures changes, making it possible to restore to any arbitrary moment within that retention window. This gives database administrators the precise, second-level granularity the policy demands.
Scheduling daily backups and keeping them for 30 days only provides restore points at daily intervals, not at the level of individual seconds. High availability with automatic failover addresses uptime and redundancy concerns but has nothing to do with recovering historical data to a specific timestamp. Enabling binary logging alone is only part of what makes point-in-time recovery work, and adjusting undo log flags does not control how long transaction logs are retained or enable granular recovery. None of these alternatives deliver the continuous, second-by-second restoration capability across a 30-day span that the scenario requires.

questions.text: A fintech company runs a transactional processing system backed by a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in the europe-west1 region. Regulatory requirements mandate that the company must be able to resume database operations in a separate geographic region within a short recovery window if europe-west1 experiences a complete outage. The team needs to select an approach that minimizes both data loss and recovery time. Which strategy best meets these requirements?

options.text: Configure high availability on the Cloud SQL instance with a failover replica in another zone within europe-west1
options.explanation:
The most effective strategy here is to leverage a cross-region read replica that can be promoted during a regional disaster. Cloud SQL supports asynchronous replication to read replicas in different regions, which keeps a near-current copy of the data available outside the primary region. If the primary region becomes completely unavailable, the team can promote the replica in the secondary region to become a standalone instance capable of accepting both read and write traffic. Although the promotion process requires manual or automated intervention and involves a brief period of unavailability, it provides the fastest and most reliable path to restoring operations compared to the other options. This pattern is widely adopted when regulatory or business continuity requirements demand rapid failover across geographic boundaries.
Enabling high availability within the same region only protects against zonal failures, so it would not help if the entire europe-west1 region went offline. Scheduling automated exports to a multi-regional Cloud Storage bucket provides a backup mechanism, but restoring from an export requires provisioning a new instance and importing the data, which results in significantly longer recovery time and greater potential data loss since exports are only as current as the last scheduled run. Running an external replica on a Compute Engine instance introduces operational complexity and removes the managed database benefits that Cloud SQL provides, making the failover process during a real disaster more error-prone and harder to execute reliably under pressure.

questions.text: A financial technology startup is preparing to release a real-time stock trading platform. During market open and close hours, they anticipate extreme and unpredictable surges in transaction volume hitting their Cloud Spanner database. The engineering team needs to ensure consistent low-latency performance during these volatile periods without over-provisioning resources during off-peak hours. Which approach should they take?

options.text: Configure the Spanner instance with the built-in autoscaling capability and set a defined maximum node limit
options.explanation:
The best approach is to leverage Cloud Spanner's native autoscaling functionality, which dynamically adjusts the number of processing units or nodes in response to real-time CPU utilization and storage metrics. This means the database can seamlessly scale up when transaction volume spikes during market open and close, and then scale back down during quieter periods to avoid unnecessary cost. Setting a maximum node limit gives the team a cost ceiling while still allowing the system to respond elastically to unpredictable demand patterns.
Provisioning additional read-only replicas helps distribute read queries but does not increase the write throughput of the instance, which is the primary concern when handling surges of trading transactions. Using sequentially incrementing primary keys is actually an anti-pattern in Cloud Spanner because it causes new rows to concentrate on a single split, creating a hotspot that degrades write performance significantly. Manually partitioning data across multiple independent Spanner instances adds substantial operational complexity and eliminates many of the advantages of Cloud Spanner as a globally distributed, strongly consistent database. None of these alternatives address the core requirement of dynamically scaling write capacity in response to unpredictable traffic bursts.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company is migrating its multi-tenant SaaS platform from on-premises MySQL databases to a single Cloud SQL for MySQL instance. The platform serves hundreds of clinics, each with dedicated table structures, resulting in roughly 120,000 tables across the combined schema. Performance monitoring from the existing environment shows that the application averages around 12 vCPUs and 55 GB of RAM during peak hours. Which Cloud SQL instance configuration should you choose to maintain operational stability and align with Google recommended best practices?

options.text: A Cloud SQL instance with 32 vCPUs and 256 GB of RAM
options.explanation:
The right choice here is the instance with 32 vCPUs and 256 GB of RAM because Cloud SQL for MySQL imposes specific resource requirements tied to the number of tables in the database, independent of active CPU or memory utilization. When the table count exceeds 50,000, the overhead associated with metadata management, the InnoDB data dictionary, and the table definition cache grows substantially. Google recommends a minimum of 32 vCPUs and at least 200 GB of memory when running more than 100,000 tables to prevent instability, slow restarts, and out-of-memory failures during schema operations. The 32 vCPU and 256 GB option meets this threshold while being the most economical choice that satisfies the requirement.
The 16 vCPU and 64 GB option appears attractive based on the observed workload averages, but it falls well below the minimum resource floor that Google specifies when table counts exceed 100,000. Running 120,000 tables on that machine size would likely result in severe performance degradation and potential crashes during routine operations like restarts or DDL statements. The 64 vCPU and 480 GB option would certainly be stable, but it provides far more compute and memory than the metadata overhead demands at this table count, leading to significant unnecessary cost. Similarly, the 96 vCPU and 624 GB option vastly exceeds what is needed and would represent a poor use of budget without delivering meaningful stability improvements over the 32 vCPU tier.

questions.text: A fintech startup runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL database that processes thousands of payment transactions per minute during peak hours. The database team has been asked to design a recovery strategy that enables restoration to any precise moment within the past seven days without degrading write throughput on the production instance. What is the best approach to meet this requirement?

options.text: Schedule serverless export jobs every two hours and store the output files in a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket
options.explanation:
The best approach is to enable automated backups along with point-in-time recovery on the Cloud SQL instance with a retention window of seven days. Cloud SQL automated backups use lightweight snapshots that avoid locking tables, which means the production instance can continue handling high-volume writes without performance degradation. Point-in-time recovery works by leveraging binary logs that record every transaction, allowing an administrator to restore the database to any specific second within the retention window. Setting the retention period to seven days ensures that both the base snapshots and the binary logs are available to cover the full recovery requirement. This is a built-in Cloud SQL capability that requires minimal operational overhead to maintain.
Running on-demand backups every four hours creates gaps between snapshots and does not provide second-level granularity because there are no binary logs being retained to fill those gaps. Exporting data from a read replica to Cloud Storage on an hourly schedule produces flat files that represent the database state at discrete intervals, making it impossible to recover to an arbitrary second without extensive manual reconstruction. Serverless exports every two hours suffer from the same limitation since they capture periodic snapshots of data rather than maintaining a continuous transaction log, and they are better suited to data portability tasks than to precise disaster recovery scenarios.

questions.text: A multinational retail company operates a Cloud SQL for MySQL database that supports its real-time inventory and order management system across multiple storefronts. The database experiences heavy transactional load around the clock and must meet a 99.99% availability target. The engineering team is concerned about the impact of Google-initiated maintenance events, which currently cause noticeable disruptions to active transactions. They need a solution that reduces maintenance-related downtime to near-zero without requiring changes to the application code or connection logic. What should you do?

options.text: Adjust the maintenance window to occur during off-peak hours
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to move the Cloud SQL instance to the Enterprise Plus edition. This edition is specifically built to support mission-critical databases that demand a 99.99% availability SLA. A key technical benefit of Enterprise Plus is its ability to complete maintenance updates with near-zero downtime, typically under 10 seconds. This is made possible through optimized failover mechanisms and tighter integration with the underlying infrastructure, which are not available in the standard Enterprise tier. Because this capability is handled entirely at the infrastructure level, the application layer does not need any modifications to benefit from it.
Enabling Point-in-Time Recovery with extended log retention is a valuable data protection strategy that allows you to restore a database to a specific moment in time, but it does nothing to keep the database available during a scheduled maintenance event. Setting up a cross-region read replica can help with disaster recovery and distributing read traffic geographically, but it does not change how the primary instance behaves when maintenance is applied to it. Adjusting the maintenance window to align with off-peak hours may reduce the business impact of downtime by shifting when it occurs, but it does not actually shorten the duration of the maintenance-related interruption itself.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Google Cloud Bigtable cluster to support its fraud detection platform, which processes millions of transactions per second with strict sub-millisecond latency requirements. The operations team recently discovered that the cluster is provisioned with significantly more nodes than typical utilization metrics suggest are necessary, resulting in infrastructure costs that are well above the approved quarterly budget. You need to bring spending under control while maintaining the same level of performance and response time guarantees. Which action should you take?

options.text: Combine several individual tables into one unified wide-column table structure
options.explanation:
The most effective way to reduce Bigtable costs without sacrificing performance is to examine how the application reads and writes data and then adjust the schema accordingly. Poorly designed row keys can create hotspots where a small number of nodes handle a disproportionate share of traffic while other nodes sit underutilized. By analyzing access patterns and redesigning the row key structure, you can distribute load more evenly across the cluster. Once the workload is balanced, it often becomes clear that fewer nodes can handle the same throughput, allowing you to safely scale down the cluster. This approach directly targets the inefficiency that causes over-provisioning and delivers cost savings without compromising latency or reliability.
Switching from SSD to HDD storage would reduce storage costs but would dramatically increase read latency, which is incompatible with a fraud detection system that demands sub-millisecond response times. Removing replica clusters from secondary regions would decrease the total node count and save money, but it would also weaken availability and disaster recovery posture, which is especially risky in financial services where uptime is critical. Merging multiple tables into a single wide-column table does not inherently improve how nodes process requests or reduce the number of nodes needed, and it can actually complicate access patterns and make future schema changes more difficult. Only by understanding the actual data distribution and optimizing the schema can you confidently reduce the node count while preserving the performance characteristics the platform requires.

questions.text: A nationwide healthcare network runs multiple on-premises MySQL 8.0 databases that power patient scheduling systems and electronic health record queries across hundreds of clinics. Each database exceeds 4 TB in size and supports read-intensive workloads with thousands of concurrent lookup queries. The systems cannot tolerate extended downtime because clinics operate around the clock. The team wants to migrate these databases to Google Cloud while preserving the current MySQL engine and achieving optimal read throughput with minimal manual effort. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.text: Use Database Migration Service to migrate to Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to leverage Database Migration Service with Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus as the target. Database Migration Service is purpose-built to handle MySQL lift-and-shift migrations with minimal downtime by using native replication to keep the source and destination synchronized until cutover. Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus includes an accelerated data cache that dramatically improves read performance, which directly addresses the read-heavy nature of patient scheduling lookups and health record queries across hundreds of clinics. Because the target remains a MySQL 8.0 engine, no application code or schema changes are needed, and the migration requires the least manual configuration of any option presented.
Datastream is a change data capture and replication service typically used to feed data into analytics platforms or BigQuery, not to orchestrate a full database migration with lifecycle management and cutover capabilities. Additionally, targeting the standard Enterprise tier of Cloud SQL would not deliver the same read performance benefits that the Enterprise Plus tier provides through its data caching layer. Using the spanner-migration-tool to move into Cloud Spanner would require significant schema redesign because Spanner uses a fundamentally different data model and query dialect, which violates the requirement to preserve the existing MySQL architecture. AlloyDB is a PostgreSQL-compatible database, so migrating a MySQL workload to AlloyDB would demand extensive refactoring of application queries and stored procedures, making it an impractical choice when the goal is to maintain the current MySQL engine with minimal effort.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company runs a PostgreSQL-based patient records system on-premises and is planning a lift-and-shift migration to Google Cloud. The compliance team requires that the database remain available even if an entire Google Cloud region goes offline. Their disaster recovery plan specifies a recovery time objective (RTO) of 30 minutes and a recovery point objective (RPO) of 45 minutes. Which approach best satisfies these resilience requirements while keeping the solution fully managed?

options.text: Migrate the PostgreSQL workload to a multi-region Cloud Spanner deployment and refactor the application layer to support the new database interface
options.explanation:
The best approach is to combine Cloud SQL regional high availability with a cross-region read replica. Regional high availability uses synchronous replication between zones, which means the database automatically fails over to a standby instance if the primary zone experiences an outage. This handles zonal failures without any manual intervention. The cross-region read replica continuously receives data through asynchronous replication, maintaining a near-current copy of the database in a geographically separate location. If the entire primary region becomes unavailable, the team can promote this replica to a standalone primary instance, restoring service well within the 30-minute RTO. Because the replica streams changes continuously rather than relying on periodic snapshots, the RPO stays comfortably within the 45-minute threshold.
Restoring from scheduled backups stored in another region would require provisioning a new Cloud SQL instance and importing what could be a very large dataset, which would almost certainly exceed the 30-minute RTO. Backups taken on a schedule also introduce a much wider gap in data currency, likely violating the RPO requirement. Deploying multiple read replicas within a single region provides no protection against a regional outage, since all replicas would go down simultaneously if that region fails. It also does not include the automatic failover mechanism that high availability provides during a zonal failure. Moving to Cloud Spanner would deliver strong multi-region resilience, but it requires significant application refactoring because Spanner uses a different SQL dialect and data model than PostgreSQL, making it impractical as a lift-and-shift migration strategy.

questions.text: A multinational financial services company operates a mission-critical Oracle database that powers its real-time trading platform. The company plans to migrate this database to AlloyDB on Google Cloud to leverage its advanced analytical capabilities and elastic scalability. The Oracle database includes extensive PL/SQL stored procedures, materialized views, and custom database-level triggers that must be carefully converted to PostgreSQL syntax. Because the trading platform serves clients across multiple time zones around the clock, the migration must ensure near-zero downtime during the final switchover. Which migration strategy should the company adopt?

options.text: Run the schema conversion through ora2pg and complete the data transfer with a one-time Database Migration Service job
options.explanation:
The Database Migration Service conversion workspace paired with a continuous migration job is the right approach here. The conversion workspace is the modern, native Google Cloud tool designed to automate the translation of complex Oracle schema objects like PL/SQL stored procedures, materialized views, and triggers into PostgreSQL-compatible equivalents. It provides a unified, managed experience that handles the structural transformation within the same platform that orchestrates the data movement. By configuring a continuous migration job, the company enables change data capture, which keeps the AlloyDB destination synchronized with the Oracle source in real time. This means the trading platform can continue operating normally while the migration progresses, and the final cutover window shrinks to seconds or minutes rather than hours.
Using ora2pg to manage both schema and data migration is a labor-intensive approach that lacks the managed automation, real-time monitoring, and tight AlloyDB integration that native Google Cloud services provide. While ora2pg is a capable open-source tool, it introduces significant manual overhead that is risky in a high-stakes financial environment. The legacy conversion tool within Database Migration Service is an outdated option that does not offer the same depth of schema mapping or the streamlined integration found in the current conversion workspace. Choosing ora2pg alongside a one-time Database Migration Service job creates two problems. First, it fragments the workflow across separate tools. Second, a one-time job requires the source database to remain static during the entire data copy, which would force extended downtime on a platform that must remain available around the clock.

questions.text: A large hospital network operates a PostgreSQL-based electronic health records system on dedicated servers in their private data center. Due to strict patient data sovereignty laws, no data can leave the physical premises under any circumstances. The IT leadership wants to modernize the database layer using an open-source compatible engine that can handle both real-time transactional queries from clinicians and complex analytical reporting. The solution must include automatic failover to ensure continuous availability during hardware failures. Which approach best satisfies all of these requirements?

options.text: Install AlloyDB Omni on a standalone virtual machine within the on-premises data center
options.explanation:
The Kubernetes-managed deployment of AlloyDB Omni on-premises is the correct answer because it addresses every constraint in the scenario. AlloyDB Omni running within a Kubernetes cluster leverages the AlloyDB Omni operator to orchestrate database instances, monitor node health, and perform automatic failover when a failure is detected. This means the hospital network gets enterprise-grade high availability without relying on manual intervention. Because the entire cluster runs on the organization's own physical infrastructure, data sovereignty requirements are fully respected. AlloyDB Omni also inherits the AlloyDB engine's ability to efficiently process both OLTP and OLAP workloads, which satisfies the need to support clinician queries alongside analytical reporting.
Using Database Migration Service to move workloads to AlloyDB would place the data in Google Cloud, which directly violates the requirement that no data can leave the physical premises. Running AlloyDB Omni in a standalone Docker container on a single server provides no built-in mechanism to handle automatic failover, since there is no orchestration layer managing replicas or detecting node failures. Similarly, installing AlloyDB Omni on a standalone virtual machine lacks the infrastructure needed to coordinate failover across multiple nodes automatically. While both the Docker and standalone VM options are suitable for development or testing scenarios, neither delivers the high availability guarantees that a mission-critical health records system demands in production.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is deploying a new patient data aggregation service built on Cloud Run. The compliance team requires that the managed Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance must have no public-facing endpoint and must only be accessible through internal Google Cloud networking. The database has been set up with internal-only routing to meet this mandate. What combination of networking and connection management components should the engineering team configure so the Cloud Run service can communicate with the database while handling rapid autoscaling?

options.text: Assign a public IP to the Cloud SQL instance, enable VPC connectivity, and set up firewall rules to restrict access to Cloud Run while deploying the Cloud SQL Auth proxy
options.explanation:
The correct approach combines a private IP on the Cloud SQL instance with Private Service Access, a Serverless VPC Access connector, and connection pooling. Private Service Access allocates a private IP within the VPC address space so the database never receives a public endpoint and all traffic remains on Google's internal network. Cloud Run services are not natively attached to a customer VPC, which means they cannot reach private resources without additional configuration. The Serverless VPC Access connector bridges this gap by allowing Cloud Run to route traffic into the VPC where the private Cloud SQL instance lives. Connection pooling is essential here because Cloud Run can scale from zero to many instances in seconds, and each instance may attempt to open its own database connection. Without pooling, this burst of connections can exhaust the database's connection limit and cause failures.
Assigning a public IP to the Cloud SQL instance violates the compliance requirement regardless of whether firewall rules are in place, because the database would still have an internet-routable address. Firewall rules reduce exposure but do not eliminate it, and auditors typically flag any public endpoint on a sensitive database. The option that pairs public IP access with the Cloud SQL Auth proxy has the same fundamental problem of internet exposure, even though the proxy adds authentication and encryption benefits. The option that uses private networking with the Cloud SQL Auth proxy instead of a connection pool addresses the network isolation requirement correctly but does not solve the connection management challenge. The Auth proxy handles secure identity-based authentication and TLS but does not multiplex or reuse connections, so a rapidly scaling serverless workload can still overwhelm the database with too many simultaneous connections.

The correct approach combines a private IP on the Cloud SQL instance with Private Service Access, a Serverless VPC Access connector, and connection pooling. Private Service Access allocates a private IP within the VPC address space so the database never receives a public endpoint and all traffic remains on Google's internal network. Cloud Run services are not natively attached to a customer VPC, which means they cannot reach private resources without additional configuration. The Serverless VPC Access connector bridges this gap by allowing Cloud Run to route traffic into the VPC where the private Cloud SQL instance lives. Connection pooling is essential here because Cloud Run can scale from zero to many instances in seconds, and each instance may attempt to open its own database connection. Without pooling, this burst of connections can exhaust the database's connection limit and cause failures.
Assigning a public IP to the Cloud SQL instance violates the compliance requirement regardless of whether firewall rules are in place, because the database would still have an internet-routable address. Firewall rules reduce exposure but do not eliminate it, and auditors typically flag any public endpoint on a sensitive database. The option that pairs public IP access with the Cloud SQL Auth proxy has the same fundamental problem of internet exposure, even though the proxy adds authentication and encryption benefits. The option that uses private networking with the Cloud SQL Auth proxy instead of a connection pool addresses the network isolation requirement correctly but does not solve the connection management challenge. The Auth proxy handles secure identity-based authentication and TLS but does not multiplex or reuse connections, so a rapidly scaling serverless workload can still overwhelm the database with too many simultaneous connections.

questions.text: A healthcare company has deployed its patient records application on AlloyDB and set up IAM database authentication so that analysts can log in using their Google Cloud identities. The alloydb.databaseUser role has been granted to each analyst's IAM account, and the iam_auth database flag is enabled on the AlloyDB instance. Analysts confirm they can authenticate and connect to the database without issues, but when they attempt to query tables in the clinical_data schema, they encounter permission denied errors. What is the appropriate way to resolve this problem?

options.text: Assign the alloydb.admin role to the analyst IAM accounts at the project level
options.explanation:
The issue here is a distinction between cloud-level IAM permissions and database-engine-level permissions. IAM roles like alloydb.databaseUser control whether a user can authenticate to the AlloyDB instance, but they do not govern what that user can do inside the PostgreSQL engine once connected. After IAM authentication succeeds, the database engine treats the user as a PostgreSQL role that has no privileges on any objects by default. You need to execute SQL GRANT statements to explicitly give these roles SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or other permissions on the specific schemas and tables they need to access. This is the standard PostgreSQL authorization model and it applies even when IAM federation handles the authentication step.
Granting the alloydb.admin role would give analysts broad control over AlloyDB resources like clusters and backups through the Google Cloud control plane, but it would not change anything about their privileges inside the database engine itself. Adding the serviceusage.apiAdmin role governs access to Google Cloud API configurations and has no relationship to SQL-level object permissions within a database. Applying the alloydb.client role at the organization level would affect the ability to connect to the instance through the AlloyDB API, but the analysts can already connect successfully, so connectivity is not the problem. None of these cloud IAM role changes would resolve permission denied errors that originate from the PostgreSQL authorization layer.

questions.text: A logistics company operates a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that powers its fleet management application. The instance was originally configured with a large machine type to accommodate anticipated growth, but after six months of operation the database consistently uses only a fraction of its allocated vCPU and memory resources. The operations team has noticed that monthly Cloud SQL costs are significantly higher than expected and wants to right-size the instance. You need to optimize costs while ensuring the database continues to perform well during periods of high demand. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a Cloud Scheduler job to invoke a Cloud Function that shuts down the instance during off-peak hours and restarts it before business hours
options.explanation:
The overprovisioned instance recommender in Cloud SQL examines historical resource utilization over time and generates specific machine type suggestions that match your actual workload. This approach eliminates guesswork when downsizing because the recommendations are based on real usage data, including peak periods. By applying these suggestions, you can confidently reduce your machine tier to one that still handles high-demand periods without degrading performance. This is the most reliable and cost-effective strategy when an instance has been provisioned beyond what the workload actually requires.
Scheduling the instance to stop and start introduces risk of data unavailability if workloads extend beyond expected hours, and it does not address the fact that the machine type is still oversized during running hours. Migrating to a smaller machine type with reduced memory without data-driven analysis can lead to performance problems such as excessive disk swapping, and automatic storage increase only manages disk capacity rather than the vCPU and memory costs that are the primary concern here. Adding a read replica in another region increases overall infrastructure costs by running a second instance and introduces cross-region network latency, which would likely hurt application performance rather than help it.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics startup runs a collection of Go and Python microservices on Google Kubernetes Engine. The team is deploying a new AlloyDB for PostgreSQL cluster to support patient outcome dashboards. The company's security team has mandated that no static database passwords may exist in Kubernetes secrets, configuration files, or hardcoded in source code. The engineering team must ensure that database connections are established without any long-lived credentials. What is the most appropriate way to set up the database connectivity?

options.text: Enable SSL on the AlloyDB instance and supply a downloaded service account key file to authenticate
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to use the AlloyDB Connector alongside IAM database authentication. When IAM authentication is enabled, the connector automatically obtains short-lived OAuth 2.0 tokens tied to the workload's attached service account rather than relying on any stored password. This means there is nothing to embed in Kubernetes secrets or inject through environment variables. The token is generated dynamically at connection time and expires quickly, so even if intercepted it has a very limited window of usefulness. This satisfies the security mandate because no static credential ever exists anywhere in the system.
Passing a Kubernetes service account name as a database username does not perform actual IAM-based token exchange and still requires some credential or password to complete the authentication handshake, which violates the policy. Routing connections through Identity-Aware Proxy can help reach a private endpoint, but it operates at the network layer and does not replace the need to present valid database credentials during the login process. Enabling SSL on the AlloyDB instance encrypts traffic between the client and the database, which protects data in transit, but a downloaded service account key file is itself a long-lived static credential that would need to be stored somewhere accessible to the application, directly contradicting the security requirement.

questions.text: A financial technology company runs a Cloud Spanner instance that powers its real-time payment processing system. After onboarding several new enterprise clients, the volume of transaction records stored in the database has grown rapidly, and monitoring alerts show that storage consumption has reached 80% of the maximum allowed capacity. The engineering team needs to act quickly to prevent write failures as more data continues to flow in throughout the business quarter. Following Google Cloud recommended practices, what should the team do to expand the storage limit of the Cloud Spanner instance?

options.text: Create an additional read-only replica in a separate Google Cloud zone
options.explanation:
The most effective approach is to scale up the compute allocation on the Cloud Spanner instance, which directly raises the storage ceiling. In Cloud Spanner, the maximum amount of data an instance can hold is determined by the number of processing units or nodes assigned to it. Adding processing units is a straightforward operation that takes effect almost immediately, allowing the database to continue accepting writes without interruption. This is the standard mechanism recommended by Google Cloud when storage utilization is approaching its limit.
Migrating to a multi-regional configuration changes how data is replicated across regions and improves global availability, but it does not alter the fundamental relationship between compute capacity and storage limits. Archiving older records to Cloud Storage could reduce the data footprint over time, but building and running such a pipeline during a period of rapid data growth introduces risk and latency that could affect the payment processing workload. Deploying a read-only replica in another zone improves fault tolerance and can offload read traffic, but replicas do not increase the storage quota available to the primary instance. Only increasing the compute resources directly expands how much data the instance is permitted to store.

questions.text: A financial services company maintains a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database that stores transaction summaries. The data governance team requires an automated process that exports specific table data every Monday at 2:00 AM to a Coldline Storage bucket so that external auditors can review it quarterly. You want to minimize infrastructure management and use fully managed Google Cloud services to orchestrate the recurring export. Which architecture should you implement?

options.text: Create a Cloud Monitoring alert policy that triggers a gcloud storage cp command based on a recurring metric threshold
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to chain together Cloud Scheduler, Pub/Sub, and a Cloud Run function to create a fully managed, serverless pipeline. Cloud Scheduler serves as a managed cron service that can reliably fire at a defined schedule such as every Monday at 2:00 AM. It publishes a message to a Pub/Sub topic, which then triggers a Cloud Run function containing the logic to call the Cloud SQL Admin API and initiate the export to the specified bucket. This architecture requires no server management, scales automatically, and only incurs cost when the function actually executes. It is the most operationally efficient way to handle a recurring database export task on Google Cloud.
Scheduling a cron job on a Compute Engine instance would technically accomplish the goal, but it requires you to maintain a virtual machine around the clock just to run a single weekly command, adding patching and availability concerns that are unnecessary. Enabling the automated backup feature in Cloud SQL is designed to create internal recovery snapshots and does not produce a user-accessible export file in a Storage bucket that external auditors could directly consume. Setting up a Cloud Monitoring alert policy is intended to respond to observed metric conditions and performance anomalies, not to execute time-based administrative workflows on a fixed weekly schedule.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm is preparing to move its on-premises PostgreSQL database to Cloud SQL. The database stores protected health information and must remain inaccessible from the public internet. The team also wants to eliminate static database credentials in favor of identity-driven access controls tied to Google Cloud permissions. Which two steps should the architect take to satisfy these requirements?

options.text: Create a Private Service Connect endpoint to reach the Cloud SQL instance
options.explanation:
Creating a Private Service Connect endpoint provides a private network path between the firm's environment and the Cloud SQL instance, ensuring that protected health information never traverses the public internet. This removes the database from any externally routable address space, which is essential when handling sensitive healthcare data. Pairing this with IAM database authentication replaces traditional username and password credentials with short-lived tokens that are governed by Google Cloud IAM policies. Restricting the instance to an internal IP address adds another layer by ensuring that only resources within the VPC or networks connected through private connectivity can initiate a connection. Together, these two measures deliver both network isolation and identity-based access control.
Configuring the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy with an external IP still exposes the instance to the public internet, which directly contradicts the requirement to keep the database inaccessible from outside the private network. Even with firewall rules, having a public endpoint increases the attack surface unnecessarily. Setting up continuous log exports to BigQuery supports auditing and observability but does nothing to secure the network path or replace static credentials. The Transfer Appliance is designed to physically ship large datasets into Google Cloud and does not address how the Cloud SQL instance is accessed or authenticated after migration. Neither logging exports nor physical transfer tools fulfill the stated needs around private connectivity and identity-driven access.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company operates a patient management system backed by a PostgreSQL database in a co-located data center. The application handles a large number of concurrent transactional operations including appointment scheduling, prescription processing, and billing updates throughout the day. At the same time, the operations team needs to run complex analytical queries against live patient and billing data to generate real-time utilization reports and cost trend dashboards. Currently, data is replicated nightly to a separate reporting database, which causes delays in reporting accuracy and adds maintenance burden on the engineering team. The company plans to migrate to Google Cloud and wants to eliminate the need to maintain separate systems by using one managed database service that natively supports both high-throughput transactional processing and analytical querying on the same dataset. Which Google Cloud database service should the company select?

options.text: BigQuery
options.explanation:
AlloyDB with PostgreSQL compatibility is the right choice here because it was built to handle demanding transactional workloads alongside analytical queries on the same operational data. Since the company already runs PostgreSQL, migrating to AlloyDB reduces compatibility concerns and simplifies the transition. AlloyDB's columnar engine accelerates analytical queries without requiring data to be exported or replicated to a separate reporting system. This directly solves the company's challenge of running real-time dashboards and reports against live transactional data in a single managed service.
Cloud SQL with PostgreSQL compatibility supports standard transactional workloads well but lacks the architectural optimizations needed to efficiently run heavy analytical queries alongside high-volume transactions on the same instance. Cloud Spanner provides global distribution and horizontal scalability with strong consistency, but it does not offer PostgreSQL compatibility and is not purpose-built to unify transactional and analytical processing in the same way. BigQuery excels at large-scale analytical processing across massive datasets, but it cannot serve as a primary transactional database handling high-concurrency writes like appointment scheduling and billing updates. Each of these alternatives covers only part of what the company needs, and none of them satisfies the combined requirement of PostgreSQL-compatible transactional and analytical processing within a single database service.

questions.text: A financial technology company operates a fraud detection engine that processes millions of transactions per second across multiple geographic regions. The system demands extremely low latency reads and writes with no tolerance for downtime during zone-level infrastructure failures. Traffic patterns are unpredictable, with sharp surges during peak shopping hours and flash sales. You need to design the data storage layer on Google Cloud using Bigtable so that the platform maintains continuous availability and automatically adapts to load changes. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy multiple independent Bigtable instances and manage data partitioning through custom application logic
options.explanation:
The best approach is to provision a Bigtable instance that includes clusters distributed across different zones while also implementing performance monitoring. When you configure multiple clusters within a single Bigtable instance, replication automatically keeps data synchronized across those clusters. Combined with a multi-cluster routing policy, this allows your application to seamlessly fail over to another zone if one becomes unavailable, which directly addresses the requirement of zero tolerance for zone-level outages. Performance monitoring ensures you can track latency and throughput metrics so that you can scale the number of nodes appropriately as traffic fluctuates during peak periods.
Deploying multiple independent Bigtable instances and handling data distribution in custom application code adds significant operational complexity without the built-in replication and automatic failover that a multi-cluster instance provides. Creating a single cluster with many nodes increases raw processing capacity but leaves the entire system vulnerable to a single zone failure, which contradicts the continuous availability requirement. A large node count alone does not provide structural redundancy across zones. Configuring a cluster in one zone and optimizing client-side write buffers may offer marginal throughput improvements under normal conditions, but it does nothing to protect against a zonal outage and does not address the need for geographic resilience in the storage tier.

questions.text: A transportation logistics company is migrating its real-time shipment tracking platform to a single-region Google Cloud Spanner instance to handle growing demand. Based on load testing, the platform must sustain a peak throughput of 28,000 read operations per second and 9,500 write operations per second with an average row size of 1 KB. The engineering team wants to provision the minimum number of nodes that will reliably handle this peak workload without wasting budget on excess capacity. How many nodes should the Spanner instance be provisioned with?

options.text: Provision a 7-node Spanner instance
options.explanation:
## Explanation
The correct number of nodes is five because the write workload is the constraining factor in this scenario. Each Cloud Spanner node in a single-region configuration can handle approximately 10,000 reads per second and 2,000 writes per second when row sizes are around 1 KB. To support 28,000 reads per second, the instance would need 2.8 nodes worth of read capacity. To support 9,500 writes per second, the instance would need 4.75 nodes worth of write capacity. Since both demands must be met simultaneously, you provision based on whichever requirement is higher, which in this case is the write throughput. Rounding 4.75 up to the next whole number gives five nodes, which provides enough capacity to handle the peak write load while also comfortably covering the read demand.
A three-node instance would support only 6,000 writes per second, which falls well short of the 9,500 writes per second the platform requires during peak traffic. A four-node instance would provide 8,000 writes per second, still insufficient to meet the 9,500 write demand and would risk degraded performance or increased latency during peak periods. A seven-node instance would handle the workload without any issues but would leave a significant amount of unused compute capacity, making it a poor choice when cost efficiency is a stated goal. Five nodes is the smallest configuration that satisfies both the read and write throughput requirements while keeping spending aligned with actual demand.

questions.text: Your company runs a microservices architecture where multiple independent services connect to a shared Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance. Recently, users have reported intermittent slowness, and your team suspects that certain services are issuing expensive queries that degrade overall database performance. You need a way to identify which specific service is responsible and drill down into the individual queries causing the problem. What should you use?

options.text: Cloud SQL instance monitoring available in the Google Cloud Console
options.explanation:
Query Insights is the right tool here because it is purpose-built to help you understand query-level performance within Cloud SQL. It allows you to tag queries by application or service, which means you can filter the dashboard to see exactly which microservice is generating problematic workloads. You can visualize query load over time, identify the most resource-intensive statements, and correlate performance degradation with specific application traffic patterns. This makes it straightforward to move from a symptom like intermittent slowness to a root cause tied to a particular service and query.
The instance monitoring page in the Google Cloud Console gives you a general health overview of your Cloud SQL database, such as connection counts and storage usage, but it does not break down performance by individual queries or connecting applications. Cloud Monitoring tracks infrastructure-level metrics like CPU utilization, memory consumption, and disk throughput, which can tell you that something is wrong but cannot point you to the specific SQL statements or services responsible. Log output from Cloud SQL can surface slow query entries, but parsing raw logs to attribute queries to specific microservices and visualize trends over time is manual and inefficient compared to a dedicated dashboard with built-in tagging and filtering capabilities.
