questions.text: Your company operates a Cloud Bigtable instance that powers a customer-facing recommendation engine with strict low-latency requirements. The data science team wants to run large historical trend analysis jobs against the same dataset stored in this instance. You need to ensure that these resource-intensive analytical queries do not degrade the response times experienced by end users of the recommendation engine. What should you do?

options.text: Modify the existing default app profile to enable multi-cluster routing across all available clusters
options.explanation:
Cloud Bigtable supports adding multiple clusters to a single instance, which means the data is automatically replicated across clusters without requiring external pipelines. By creating a dedicated application profile that routes the data science team's analytical traffic to the new cluster, you establish workload isolation. The recommendation engine continues to operate against the original cluster without contention from heavy read-intensive batch queries. Application profiles give you fine-grained control over which cluster handles which type of traffic, making this the standard pattern in Cloud Bigtable when you need to separate real-time serving from analytical processing.
Creating a separate instance and using Dataflow to replicate the data introduces unnecessary operational complexity and data synchronization challenges when Bigtable already handles replication natively across clusters within a single instance. Changing the default app profile to multi-cluster routing would spread all traffic across clusters indiscriminately, which means analytical queries could still land on the same cluster as the recommendation engine and cause latency issues. Increasing the node count and enabling autoscaling adds more capacity to a single cluster, but a heavy batch scan can still consume disproportionate resources and affect real-time queries sharing those same nodes. Autoscaling may also respond too slowly to sudden bursts of analytical activity to prevent momentary latency spikes in the recommendation engine.

questions.text: A logistics company runs a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database that powers its order tracking system. During business hours, dispatchers and warehouse staff report that certain updates to shipment records hang or take significantly longer than expected. The operations team suspects that overlapping write transactions may be causing delays, and they also want to confirm whether the database instance itself is under resource pressure. What combination of tools and configurations should you use to identify the source of these performance problems?

options.text: Review System Insights, ensure the log_lock_waits flag is enabled, and review Cloud Monitoring to check CPU utilization and connection counts
options.explanation:
The best approach is to use System Insights alongside the log_lock_waits database flag and Cloud Monitoring resource metrics. System Insights gives you a dedicated dashboard that surfaces database-level performance data and helps you spot bottlenecks such as lock contention. Enabling log_lock_waits is the critical step here because it causes PostgreSQL to log whenever a session waits longer than the deadlock timeout to acquire a lock. This directly addresses the suspicion that overlapping write transactions are blocking each other. Once those lock wait events are being logged, you can correlate them with CPU utilization and active connection counts in Cloud Monitoring to determine whether the instance is running out of compute capacity or whether the problem is purely a concurrency issue in the application logic.
Using log_min_duration_statement with Cloud Trace would help you find slow individual queries and trace latency through the application stack, but it does not reveal whether those slow queries are caused by lock contention between concurrent transactions. Querying pg_stat_activity and checking storage capacity can give you a snapshot of current sessions and disk usage, but neither provides the historical logging of lock waits needed to catch intermittent blocking that happens unpredictably during peak hours. Configuring Query Insights and enabling log_connections would show you popular query patterns and new connection events, but these do not capture wait events or blocking relationships between transactions, which is the specific diagnostic information the scenario requires. Memory exhaustion searches would be relevant if the instance were crashing or running out of memory, not when transactions are waiting on each other.

questions.text: A logistics company maintains its shipment tracking records in a Cloud Spanner database. The business intelligence team occasionally needs to run complex aggregation queries across large volumes of historical shipment data. The company wants to keep costs as low as possible while ensuring that only authorized analysts can access the data. What is the recommended architecture to support this use case?

options.text: Create a view directly within the Spanner database and grant the data analyst IAM role to the appropriate user group
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use BigQuery federated queries to read directly from the Spanner database without moving or duplicating the data. Because the business intelligence team only runs these analytical queries occasionally, there is no need to maintain a continuously updated copy of the data in a separate location. Federated queries allow BigQuery to reach into Spanner on demand, meaning compute costs are incurred only when a query actually executes. A BigQuery view built on top of the federated query gives analysts a clean interface to work with, and IAM permissions on both the BigQuery dataset and the connection object ensure that only authorized users can access the underlying shipment data. This provides a strong balance between cost efficiency and security.
Scheduling a nightly Dataflow batch pipeline introduces ongoing compute and storage costs that are unnecessary when query frequency is low. That approach makes more sense when data needs to be transformed or when queries run frequently enough to justify a materialized copy. Setting up a Spanner change stream to replicate data into BigQuery in near real time adds continuous processing overhead and storage duplication, which is suited to dashboards or reporting workloads that demand fresh data at all times, not occasional analysis. Creating a view directly inside the Spanner database would force the analytical workload onto the same instance that handles transactional traffic, potentially requiring a larger Spanner configuration to avoid performance degradation, and Spanner lacks the specialized analytical functions that BigQuery provides natively.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company operates a large on-premises Oracle database containing hundreds of stored procedures, triggers, and complex PL/SQL logic that supports its reporting platform. The organization has decided to migrate this workload to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL as part of a broader cloud-first initiative. The engineering team needs to convert the Oracle-specific database code to PostgreSQL-compatible syntax with as little manual effort as possible, while keeping all migration tooling within Google Cloud. Which approach best supports this migration goal?

options.text: Utilize the code conversion workspace in Database Migration Service
options.explanation:
The code conversion workspace within Database Migration Service is purpose-built to handle exactly this type of cross-engine migration challenge. It provides an automated environment that can parse dialect-specific constructs such as stored procedures and triggers from Oracle or SQL Server and produce PostgreSQL-compatible equivalents. The workspace flags areas that require manual review, allowing engineers to focus their effort on the exceptions rather than rewriting everything from scratch. This keeps the entire migration workflow inside Google Cloud and reduces the risk of human error during what would otherwise be an extremely tedious translation process.
Manually translating every stored procedure and trigger is feasible but wildly impractical when dealing with hundreds of database objects, and the likelihood of introducing subtle bugs increases with scale. Exporting the schema and data without first converting the syntax will result in immediate failures because PostgreSQL cannot interpret Oracle PL/SQL constructs natively, making this a waste of time and effort. Adding a middleware abstraction layer between the application and the database creates ongoing architectural complexity, introduces latency, and does nothing to actually convert the underlying database code to run on PostgreSQL. Each of these alternatives either shifts the problem without solving it or dramatically increases the time and risk involved in the migration compared to using the integrated tooling Google Cloud provides.

questions.text: A financial services company operates a high-volume transaction processing system on Cloud Spanner. The database was originally deployed in a single-region configuration, but due to new regulatory and disaster recovery requirements, the team moved to a multi-regional instance configuration. Since the migration, the application's read latency has stayed within acceptable limits, but end users in remote offices are reporting that transaction commits are taking noticeably longer than before. You want to address the increased write latency without reverting to a single-region deployment. What should you do?

options.text: Add additional read-write replicas to the multi-regional instance
options.explanation:
Position the write-generating application servers close to the default leader region is the correct approach to solving this problem. In a multi-regional Cloud Spanner deployment, all write operations must be coordinated through the leader replica using the Paxos consensus protocol. When application servers issuing writes are located far from the leader region, each write transaction incurs significant round-trip network latency as requests travel to the leader and acknowledgments travel back. By placing the application tier in or near the leader region, you drastically cut down the physical distance involved in this coordination, which directly reduces the time each write takes to complete. This is the primary strategy recommended by Google when teams experience degraded write performance after transitioning from a regional to a multi-regional Spanner topology.
Enabling data boost does not help here because it provides separate compute resources intended to support analytical and batch workloads without impacting production traffic, and it has no effect on standard transactional write speed. Applying bounded staleness reads is a technique that allows read operations to return slightly older data from nearby replicas, which improves read latency but does nothing to accelerate writes since writes still must go through the leader. Adding more read-write replicas would actually make the problem worse because the Paxos protocol requires a quorum of replicas to acknowledge each write, and increasing the number of participating nodes can increase the time needed to reach consensus rather than decrease it. The fundamental bottleneck in this scenario is the geographic distance between the clients issuing writes and the leader replica, and only reducing that distance addresses the root cause.

options.text: Apply bounded staleness reads with a minimum fifteen-second tolerance
options.explanation:
Position the write-generating application servers close to the default leader region is the correct approach to solving this problem. questions.text: Your team recently migrated a PostgreSQL database from an on-premises server to Cloud SQL. One of the legacy tasks was a nightly cron job that generated a summary report by querying several tables and writing the output to Cloud Storage. You need to replicate this scheduled task in Google Cloud while minimizing both infrastructure management and monthly costs. What should you do?

options.text: Set up Cloud Composer and define a DAG to run the nightly report generation
options.explanation:
The best approach here is combining Cloud Scheduler with Cloud Functions to handle the nightly report generation. Cloud Scheduler serves as a fully managed cron replacement that can trigger a Cloud Function at a specified time each night. The Cloud Function then runs the query against Cloud SQL and writes the results to Cloud Storage. Because both services are serverless, there is no underlying infrastructure to patch, update, or monitor. You only pay for the brief execution window when the function runs, which keeps costs extremely low compared to alternatives that require persistent resources.
Cloud Composer is built on Apache Airflow and is designed to orchestrate complex, multi-step workflows across many systems. It requires a persistent environment that carries a significant baseline monthly cost, making it far too expensive and complex for a single nightly task. Running a dedicated Compute Engine instance means paying for a virtual machine that sits idle for nearly 24 hours between each execution, and you also take on responsibility for OS-level maintenance and security patching. Dataflow is a data processing service optimized for large-scale batch and streaming workloads, and deploying it as a continuous pipeline to handle one simple nightly report would introduce unnecessary complexity and cost that far exceeds the requirements of this use case.

questions.text: A mid-sized financial services company is closing its co-located data center and must transition its PostgreSQL and MySQL databases to Cloud SQL. The team cannot provision additional local disk space to store database export files, and the business requires near-zero downtime throughout the migration process. Which two approaches should you recommend to migrate the databases while maintaining synchronization between the on-premises sources and the cloud targets?

options.text: Deploy an external read replica of the cloud instance
options.explanation:
The right combination here involves using Database Migration Service alongside external source server replication. Database Migration Service is a fully managed offering that orchestrates the movement of data from on-premises databases into Cloud SQL, handling both the initial data load and ongoing change capture through continuous replication. Because it streams data directly rather than requiring a full database dump to be stored locally, it satisfies the constraint of having no available disk space for export files. Setting up replication from an external source server is the complementary technical approach that allows the Cloud SQL instance to function as a replica of the on-premises database until the team is ready to perform a final cutover. Together, these two methods ensure the cloud destination stays synchronized with the production source throughout the migration window, enabling near-zero downtime.
Deploying an external read replica of the cloud instance reverses the direction of what is needed, as the goal is to replicate into Cloud SQL from an outside environment, not to create a replica outside of it. Creating a cross-region read replica is a feature designed to distribute read traffic across Google Cloud regions and assumes the primary instance already lives inside Cloud SQL, which does not apply to an on-premises source. Similarly, setting up a standard read replica on the cloud database is meant to scale read capacity within Google Cloud and does not provide connectivity or replication mechanisms to pull data from a database running in an external data center. None of these three alternatives address the specific requirement of migrating data from an on-premises environment into Cloud SQL while keeping the target synchronized with the source under tight storage and downtime constraints.

questions.text: A mid-sized e-commerce platform relies on a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in europe-west1-b as its transactional database. The team recently created a read replica in europe-west1-c dedicated to powering a customer analytics application. Leadership has raised concerns that a single zone outage could knock out the analytics application, but they do not want to substantially increase infrastructure costs. What change should be made to protect the analytics workload from a zonal disruption?

options.text: Enable the high availability configuration on the read replica
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to turn on high availability directly on the read replica. Cloud SQL supports enabling HA on read replicas independently from the primary instance. When this is configured, a standby replica is automatically maintained in a different zone within the same region. If the zone hosting the active read replica goes down, Cloud SQL performs an automatic failover to the standby, keeping the analytics application available. This adds a modest cost for the standby but avoids the significantly higher expenses that come with cross-region deployments.
Creating a read replica in a separate region would introduce cross-region egress charges and higher latency, which conflicts with the goal of keeping costs low. Promoting the read replica to a standalone primary would break the replication relationship with the original primary instance and could result in stale or diverging data, which does not solve the availability problem and creates operational complexity. Relocating the replica to the same zone as the primary instance would actually make things worse because a single zone failure would then affect both the transactional database and the analytics workload simultaneously.

questions.text: A multinational insurance company is migrating its claims processing platform from on-premises infrastructure to Google Cloud. The platform makes extensive use of advanced PostgreSQL capabilities including custom stored procedures, triggers, and complex joins. The company requires an RTO of under 10 minutes and near-zero data loss if a zone goes down. Due to strict regulatory mandates, all write transactions must remain within a single designated region in Europe. However, claims adjusters and compliance teams spread across Asia-Pacific and North America need fast read access to claims data without noticeable delays. Which solution best meets these requirements?

options.text: An AlloyDB cluster configured with high availability in the European region and cross-region read replicas
options.explanation:
The best solution here is AlloyDB with high availability in the European region combined with cross-region read replicas. AlloyDB is fully compatible with PostgreSQL, which means the company can migrate its stored procedures, triggers, and complex joins without rewriting application logic. The high-availability configuration uses a decoupled storage architecture that enables rapid failover and standby promotion, meeting the sub-10-minute RTO and near-zero RPO targets. Cross-region read replicas allow claims adjusters in Asia-Pacific and compliance teams in North America to query data with low latency, while all write operations remain confined to the designated European region to satisfy regulatory requirements.
A Cloud SQL PostgreSQL deployment with high availability and regional storage provides solid zonal failover within a single region but lacks the ability to serve low-latency reads to users on other continents, which is a core requirement in this scenario. A multi-region Cloud SQL instance with internal load balancing to distribute writes globally directly violates the regulatory mandate that all write transactions must stay within a single European region. A multi-regional Cloud Spanner instance could offer global availability, but Spanner is not PostgreSQL-compatible in the way AlloyDB is, and migrating the existing stored procedures, triggers, and complex joins would require a substantial rewrite of the application layer, making it a poor fit given the heavy reliance on advanced PostgreSQL features.

questions.text: A ride-sharing platform stores continuous GPS pings from 200,000 active drivers in a Bigtable cluster, processing up to 3 million writes per second during rush hours. The row key follows the pattern driver_id#epoch_millis, and monitoring reveals that certain tablet servers are overwhelmed while others have minimal load. The company retains all location history indefinitely, resulting in an 800TB dataset that is driving storage costs well above projections. Only the most recent 90 days of data is queried by the real-time matching engine, which requires read latency under 8ms to maintain acceptable rider wait times. What combination of changes should you make to resolve these issues?

options.text: Increase the cluster size to 30 nodes, add a salted hash prefix to row keys, and enable cross-region replication across all table families
options.explanation:
The correct approach combines a hash-based row key prefix with cluster autoscaling and offloading stale data to Cloud Storage. Bigtable distributes rows lexicographically across tablet servers, and when many driver IDs share similar prefixes or access patterns, a subset of nodes can become overloaded. Prepending a hash of the driver_id spreads writes evenly across the entire cluster, eliminating hotspots. Autoscaling allows the cluster to grow during rush-hour peaks to sustain sub-8ms read latency and then shrink during off-peak windows to control costs. Since only the last 90 days of GPS data serves the matching engine, exporting everything older to Cloud Storage dramatically reduces the 800TB footprint and the associated Bigtable storage bill without affecting live query performance.
Manually expanding to a fixed 30-node cluster may add capacity but does nothing to correct the uneven key distribution that causes hotspotting, and cross-region replication adds cost and complexity without addressing the storage budget problem. Splitting data into separate tables by geographic region introduces operational overhead and does not inherently fix write skew, while exporting cold data to BigQuery is more expensive than Cloud Storage when the data simply needs to be retained rather than frequently analyzed. Leading the row key with a timestamp is a well-known Bigtable anti-pattern because all current writes funnel into the same tablet, creating an even worse hotspot than the original design. Keeping all historical rows on SSD-backed storage is the most expensive tier Bigtable offers and directly contradicts the goal of reducing storage costs.

questions.text: A financial services company operates a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL High Availability instance that supports its real-time transaction processing platform. Earlier today, the security team flagged a critical vulnerability patch that Google has made available but scheduled to auto-apply during next week's maintenance cycle. Company policy mandates that critical vulnerability patches must be deployed within 12 hours of discovery, and the change management board has approved a maintenance slot between midnight and 2 AM tonight. How should the database engineer ensure the patch is applied across the full instance cluster within the approved timeframe?

options.text: Individually run self-service maintenance on each standby and failover replica
options.explanation:
## Explanation
The right approach here is to use the Cloud SQL self-service maintenance feature to manually initiate the update on the primary instance during the approved change window. When self-service maintenance is triggered on a Cloud SQL primary instance, the platform automatically orchestrates the patch rollout across all associated components, including high availability standby instances and any read replicas. This means the database engineer does not need to separately manage each node in the cluster. The update propagates in the correct sequence to maintain data integrity and cluster consistency. By initiating this during the approved midnight window, the engineer satisfies the company's 12-hour remediation policy while relying on Google's managed infrastructure to handle the coordination.
Switching the maintenance track to the preview channel controls which feature releases an instance receives over time and is not a mechanism to force a specific pending security patch to apply on demand. Attempting to run self-service maintenance individually on each standby or failover replica is not the intended workflow in Cloud SQL and risks creating version inconsistencies across the cluster. Changing the preferred weekly maintenance window only adjusts the recurring schedule going forward and does not cause a currently pending patch to be applied right away. The self-service maintenance trigger on the primary is the only option that both applies the patch immediately and ensures the entire cluster is updated in a coordinated manner.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics startup deploys its data processing pipeline on Google Kubernetes Engine. The pipeline connects to a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database that stores sensitive patient billing records. A recent compliance review found that the database credentials must be rotated on a regular 60-day cycle, and the rotation must be automated with no manual steps. The development team needs a way to ensure that pods running in the cluster can always fetch the latest valid password without downtime. What should you do?

options.text: Set up IAM database authentication by creating a one-to-one mapping between the application service account and a separate database user per requesting client
options.explanation:
The best approach is to leverage Secret Manager with an automated rotation workflow driven by a Cloud Function. Secret Manager provides a centralized, auditable location to hold sensitive credentials, and a Cloud Function can be triggered on a 60-day schedule to generate a new password, update it in the Cloud SQL instance, and store the new version in Secret Manager. The application pods simply call the Secret Manager API to retrieve the latest secret version, which means they always have a valid password without any manual coordination or redeployment.
Encrypting a password with Cloud KMS and baking it into the container image makes periodic rotation extremely difficult because every rotation would require rebuilding and redeploying the image across all nodes. Storing credentials in a Cloud Storage bucket introduces management overhead and does not provide any built-in mechanism to automate the rotation lifecycle on a fixed schedule. IAM database authentication uses short-lived tokens rather than traditional passwords, so it does not satisfy a compliance requirement that specifically mandates rotating a database password every 60 days. Additionally, the option describes mapping individual requesting clients to separate database users, which is not a standard or practical pattern and does not address the stated rotation requirement.

questions.text: A retail company uses Google Cloud Memorystore for Redis to cache product catalog data and user shopping cart sessions. During a seasonal sales event, customers report slow page loads and intermittent failures when adding items to their carts. Your team investigates and finds that Cloud Monitoring metrics show a significant increase in evicted keys while the number of client connections has remained consistent throughout the event. What should you do to address the root cause of these performance degradations?

options.text: Relocate the application tier to the same region as the Memorystore instance
options.explanation:
The high rate of key evictions confirms that the Redis instance has run out of available memory and is being forced to remove existing data to make room for new entries. Increasing the memory allocation on the Memorystore instance gives the cache enough room to hold the full working dataset without triggering eviction cycles. When Redis no longer needs to constantly identify and purge keys, the processing overhead that causes latency spikes disappears. A properly sized instance also maintains a much higher cache hit ratio, which means fewer requests fall through to the backend database during peak demand.
Extending the expiration duration on cached keys would make the problem worse because data would linger in memory longer and consume even more of the already insufficient space. Moving the application servers closer to the instance could reduce network round-trip time but would have no effect on the memory pressure that is causing evictions. Adding read replicas helps distribute query traffic across multiple nodes but does not expand the memory limit on the primary instance where the eviction activity is actually happening. Since the monitoring data clearly points to a memory constraint rather than a connection or network issue, only resizing the instance directly resolves the underlying problem.

questions.text: A multinational financial services firm runs trading applications across the Americas, EMEA, and APAC regions. User portfolio data is stored in Cloud Bigtable, and each region has its own application deployment. The team wants a single Bigtable instance so that data automatically replicates between regions. They need to guarantee that each regional deployment reads and writes only to the local cluster, minimizing latency and avoiding cross-region data transfer costs. Which configuration should they use?

options.text: Three separate instances with one cluster each and three app profiles each using single-cluster routing
options.explanation:
The best approach is to deploy a single Bigtable instance with clusters in each of the three regions and create three app profiles that each use single-cluster routing pointed at the local cluster. A single instance with multiple clusters gives you built-in replication, meaning data written in one region is automatically available in the others. By assigning each regional application deployment its own app profile with single-cluster routing, you pin that deployment's traffic to the nearest cluster. This eliminates cross-region reads and writes, which keeps latency low and avoids expensive inter-continental network transfer charges.
Using one app profile with multi-cluster routing would allow Bigtable to route requests to any available cluster, which means a temporary issue in one region could cause traffic to be sent to a cluster on another continent. This would introduce high latency and unexpected cross-region transfer costs, violating both stated requirements. Deploying three separate instances rather than one would break automatic replication between regions, since Bigtable replication only works between clusters within the same instance. Without replication, the team would need to build and maintain a custom synchronization mechanism, which contradicts their goal of simplifying management through a single instance. The option combining three separate instances with multi-cluster routing is also invalid because multi-cluster routing applies to clusters within a single instance, and each of those instances would only have one cluster, making that routing configuration meaningless.

This eliminates cross-region reads and writes, which keeps latency low and avoids expensive inter-continental network transfer charges.
Using one app profile with multi-cluster routing would allow Bigtable to route requests to any available cluster, which means a temporary issue in one region could cause traffic to be sent to a cluster on another continent. This would introduce high latency and unexpected cross-region transfer costs, violating both stated requirements. Deploying three separate instances rather than one would break automatic replication between regions, since Bigtable replication only works between clusters within the same instance. Without replication, the team would need to build and maintain a custom synchronization mechanism, which contradicts their goal of simplifying management through a single instance. The option combining three separate instances with multi-cluster routing is also invalid because multi-cluster routing applies to clusters within a single instance, and each of those instances would only have one cluster, making that routing configuration meaningless.

questions.text: A retail company runs its transactional database on Cloud SQL for MySQL. During peak shopping hours, the primary database instance becomes a bottleneck because thousands of concurrent analytics and reporting queries compete with write operations. The engineering team wants to offload all read traffic to multiple replicas while keeping the application configuration simple by using only one connection endpoint. What is the best approach?

options.text: Enable Cloud SQL database caching
options.explanation:
A Cloud SQL read pool is the right solution because it aggregates multiple read replicas behind a single endpoint, automatically distributing incoming read queries across all replicas in the pool. The application only needs to connect to one address, which greatly simplifies configuration and removes the need to manage individual replica connections in application code. As read demand increases during peak shopping periods, additional replicas can be added to the pool to scale horizontally without changing the connection string. This feature is purpose-built to handle scenarios where read-heavy workloads need to be distributed across several replicas using a unified entry point.
Deploying a Cloud SQL proxy instance provides secure connectivity and IAM-based authentication to a Cloud SQL instance, but it does not create or manage a set of read replicas or balance traffic among them. Configuring a high availability cluster protects against downtime by maintaining a standby instance that can take over during a failure, but it does not spread read queries across multiple nodes to increase throughput. Enabling database caching may reduce response times when the same queries are repeated frequently, but it does not add horizontal read capacity or offer a single consolidated endpoint that routes traffic to a group of replicas. None of these alternatives address the combined requirement of scaling out read operations and simplifying connectivity through one endpoint.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm needs to migrate a large clinical data warehouse from an on-premises MySQL deployment to Cloud SQL using Database Migration Service. The source database serves real-time dashboards used by hospital staff around the clock, and any degradation in query response times could delay critical patient care decisions. The engineering team wants to complete the migration in the shortest possible timeframe without affecting the production workload. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Upgrade the CPU and memory of the source primary server to absorb the additional migration overhead
options.explanation:
The best approach is to run the migration from a dedicated read replica with the highest initial load parallelism setting. A read replica absorbs the intensive data extraction work that the initial dump phase requires, which means the primary instance remains fully available to serve the real-time dashboards that hospital staff depend on. Setting parallelism to the maximum value ensures the migration uses multiple threads to transfer data concurrently, completing the bulk load phase as quickly as possible. Once the initial load finishes, Database Migration Service switches to reading the binary logs to capture ongoing changes and keep the Cloud SQL target synchronized with the source.
Launching multiple independent migration jobs targeting the same destination creates risks around data consistency and coordination, and it adds unnecessary overhead on the Cloud SQL instance as it tries to reconcile parallel writes from separate jobs. Upgrading the primary server hardware is an expensive workaround that does not eliminate the resource contention between production queries and the migration dump process. Pointing the migration directly at the primary with maximum parallelism would consume significant CPU and I/O on the production server, which could cause query timeouts and degrade dashboard performance. Throttling the migration to low parallelism during off-peak windows extends the overall migration timeline significantly, and since the dashboards are used around the clock, there is no true off-peak period that would make this strategy safe or efficient.
